validation.reason: Presentation timing duplicates the authored fractions misconception and is not an independent benchmark case.

ground_truth.basis: Multiple learners express the denominator-magnitude misconception and miss the baseline poll before a visible-model re-teach.

events.speaker: Ms. Morgan
events.text: Today we are comparing fraction size. I want you to explain your reasoning, not only choose an answer.

events.speaker: Ms. Morgan
events.text: Which is greater, one fourth or one eighth, and why?

events.speaker: Sarah
events.text: I think one eighth because eight is the bigger number.

events.speaker: Priya
events.text: I am confused. Does a bigger denominator make the fraction bigger?

events.speaker: Leo
events.text: Not sure, but I also chose one eighth.

events.speaker: Ms. Morgan
events.text: Let us draw two equal-sized fraction bars. I divide the first bar into four equal pieces and the second into eight equal pieces. Compare one piece from each bar: which piece is larger?

events.speaker: Sarah
events.text: Now I see it. Eighths are smaller because the same whole was cut into more pieces.

events.speaker: Priya
events.text: One fourth is bigger because each fourth is a larger piece than each eighth.

events.question: Follow-up check: Which is greater, 1/3 or 1/6?

events.speaker: Ms. Morgan
events.text: Explain the general relationship between equal wholes, denominator size, and piece size to a partner.
